item.content:encoded:
I honestly really enjoyed this one, although i don't personally have relationship problems with my mother this made me feel bad for the SCP and the interviewer despite me having to experience what they have went (or still going) through, so props to you.

As said above the casual interview logs are a bit of a problem for some people. For me they un-immerse me a little but this in my opinion made me see both the characters as just, well guys with mother issues, not some soulless interviewer and some SCP.

Also the death at the end seemed really out of place to me, like i was just thinking "Dam did it really just die?". Also if it is dead then why is its classification Euclid and not Neutralized?

All and all a pretty good SCP, hope this helps :P

item.title: Good story, just forgot the Foundation bit
item.content:encoded:
I don't like this one for a couple of reasons. As stated by others, the death at the end doesn't elicit the reaction I imagine you were hoping for. It was more like: "Wait he actually died?" than anything emotional.

I also really don't like the conprocs. The fact that he can literally make the entire night sky explode and the only info you give is a "disinformation campaign." That is really weak, and, in my opinion, is a cop-out in this case. -1 from me.

The ending is what got me. Take your +1 you beautiful bastard.

I get the feeling this article is somewhat personal given the context? I sometimes see people take their own life experiences and adapt it into an article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmm. Echoing the above when I say that the description has some hyper-casual verbiage that's off-putting, albeit not really a deal-breaker. The interviews are a definitely positive, and I enjoy the central character; the dialogue is a little open with the story it's presenting, and it's a tad straight-forward to have him speak English perfectly despite his injuries/alienness but, eh.

The ending is disappointing, though. I felt emotionally interested in the fate of the star-man but not enough to be moved by his death. Frankly I'd prefer a version where he doesn't die, or dies in some unusual way, instead of what was directly foreshadowed in the conprocs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While there's an emotional connection here, these interviews totally buck any and all conventional approach a scientific organization should be taking. They wildly wrench me out of immersion with their super casual language.

This lacks polish, but I recognize the underlying story. No vote from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really liked this in the draft form, and I still enjoy it. I found myself relating to the dude with his mom as someone with.. mother issues as well. Especially the last interview where he says "here's to bad moms"

I feel sad for the dude a lot; guy just wanted to make art but his mother wanted him to run track and in the result, he died away from his family and the mother lost her kid… Just… god sad

+1

item.title: Author's Post
item.content:encoded: Here's some nerds real cool people that helped me cobble this piece together. chiifu InvictusRaged Anorrack MomBun TwistedFox
